Description:
As a quick recap, Virtual Nodes lets Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) burst pods onto Azure Container Instances (ACI) on‑demand, without managing or scaling extra VM nodes.

In this article I will demonstrate how you can migrate an existing AKS cluster using the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy), to the new generation of Virtual Nodes on ACI, which is deployed and managed via Helm.

More information about Virtual Nodes on Azure Container Instances can be found [here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-virtual-nodes), and the GitHub repo is available [here](https://github.com/microsoft/VirtualNodesOnAzureContainerInstances).

Advanced documentation for Virtual Nodes on ACI is also available [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/tree/main/Docs), and includes topics such as node customisation, release notes and a troubleshooting guide.

> >
> **Please note that all code samples within this guide are examples only, and are provided without warranty/support.**
> >

## Background

Virtual Nodes on ACI is rebuilt from the ground-up, and includes several fixes and enhancements, for instance:

**Added support/features**

- VNet peering, outbound traffic to the internet with network security groups
- Init containers
- Host aliases
- Arguments for exec in ACI
- Persistent Volumes and Persistent Volume Claims
- Container hooks
- Confidential containers (see supported regions list [here](https://github.com/microsoft/VirtualNodesOnAzureContainerInstances#region-deployments))
- [ACI standby pools](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/NodeCustomizations.md#standby-pools)

**Planned future enhancements**

- Support for ACR image pull via Service Principal (SPN)
- Kubernetes network policies
- Support for IPv6
- Windows containers
- Port Forwarding

Note: The new generation of the add-on is managed via Helm rather than as an AKS managed add-on.

### Limitations

- DaemonSets are not supported
- Virtual Nodes on ACI requires AKS clusters with Azure CNI networking (Kubenet is not supported)
- Incompatible with API server authorized IP ranges for AKS (because of the subnet delegation to ACI)

### Requirements

- Each Virtual Nodes on ACI deployment requires 3 vCPUs and 12 GiB memory on one of the AKS cluster’s VMs.
- Each Virtual Nodes on ACI deployment supports up to 200 pods.

# Deploying the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy)

For the sake of completeness, I will first guide you through the traditional steps of deploying the Virtual Nodes managed add-on for AKS.

For this walkthrough, I'm using Bash via [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL),](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) along with the [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli).

These steps are adapted from the official documentation [here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/virtual-nodes-cli)

1. Set up environment variables:
2. location=northeurope
rg=rg-virtualnode-demo vnetName=vnet-virtualnode-demo clusterName=aks-virtualnode-demo aksSubnetName=subnet-aks vnSubnetName=subnet-vn
3. Create resource group for the cluster and VNet:
4. az group create --name $rg --location $location
5. Create Virtual Network (VNet) and AKS/ACI subnets:
6. az network vnet create \
--resource-group $rg --name $vnetName \ --address-prefixes 10.0.0.0/8 \ --subnet-name $aksSubnetName \ --subnet-prefix 10.240.0.0/16

az network vnet subnet create \ --resource-group $rg \ --vnet-name $vnetName \ --name $vnSubnetName \ --address-prefixes 10.241.0.0/16 \ --delegations Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups
7. Retrieve the resource IDs for the AKS and ACI subnets:
8. az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $aksSubnetName --query id -o tsv
subnetId=$(az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $aksSubnetName --query id -o tsv) vnSubnetId=$(az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $vnSubnetName --query id -o tsv)
9. Create a small AKS cluster with 2 nodes:
10. az aks create --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName \
--node-count 2 --node-osdisk-size 30 --node-vm-size Standard\_B4ms \ --network-plugin azure --vnet-subnet-id $subnetId \ --generate-ssh-keys
11. Enable the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy):
12. az aks enable-addons --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --addons virtual-node --subnet-name $vnSubnetName
13. Retrieve the Managed Identity (MSI) used by Virtual Nodes and assign it the *Network Contributor* role for the ACI subnet:
14. vnIdentityId=$(az aks show \
--resource-group $rg \ --name $clusterName \ --query "addonProfiles.aciConnectorLinux.identity.resourceId" \ -o tsv)

vnIdentityObjectId=$(az identity show --ids $vnIdentityId --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$vnIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Network Contributor" \ --scope "$vnSubnetId"
15. Download the cluster's *kubeconfig* file:
16. az aks get-credentials --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName
17. Confirm the Virtual Nodes node shows within the cluster and is in a *Ready* state (*virtual-node-aci-linux*):
18. $ kubectl get node
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000000 Ready 46m v1.33.6 aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000001 Ready 46m v1.33.6 virtual-node-aci-linux Ready agent 3m28s v1.25.0-vk-azure-aci-1.6.2

## Migrating to the next generation of Virtual Nodes on Azure Container Instances via Helm chart

I will now explain how to migrate from the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy) to the new generation of Virtual Nodes on ACI.

For this walkthrough, I'm using Bash via [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL),](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) along with the [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli).

Direct migration is not supported, and therefore the steps below show an example of removing Virtual Nodes managed add-on and its resources and then installing the Virtual Nodes on ACI Helm chart.

In this walkthrough I will explain how to delete and re-create the Virtual Nodes subnet, however if you need to preserve the VNet and/or use a custom subnet name, refer to the Helm customisation steps [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/NodeCustomizations.md#default-aci-subnet-behaviors-with-a-customized-acisubnetname).

1. Initialise environment variables
2. location=northeurope
rg=rg-virtualnode-demo vnetName=vnet-virtualnode-demo clusterName=aks-virtualnode-demo aksSubnetName=subnet-aks vnSubnetName=subnet-vn
3. Scale-down any running Virtual Nodes workloads (example below):
4. kubectl delete deploy -n
5. Disable the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy):
6. az aks disable-addons --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --addons virtual-node
7. Export a backup of the original subnet configuration:
8. az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $vnSubnetName > subnetConfigOriginal.json
9. Delete the original subnet (subnets cannot be renamed and therefore must be re-created):
10. az network vnet subnet delete -g $rg -n $vnSubnetName --vnet-name $vnetName
11. Create the new Virtual Nodes on ACI subnet (replicate the configuration of the original subnet but with the specific name value of *cg*):
12. vnSubnetId=$(az network vnet subnet create \
--resource-group $rg \ --vnet-name $vnetName \ --name cg \ --address-prefixes 10.241.0.0/16 \ --delegations Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups --query id -o tsv)
13. Assign the cluster's *-kubelet* identity *Contributor* access to the infrastructure resource group, and *Network Contributor* access to the ACI subnet:
14. nodeRg=$(az aks show --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --query nodeResourceGroup -o tsv)
nodeRgId=$(az group show -n $nodeRg --query id -o tsv)

agentPoolIdentityId=$(az aks show --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --query "identityProfile.kubeletidentity.resourceId" -o tsv)

agentPoolIdentityObjectId=$(az identity show --ids $agentPoolIdentityId --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$agentPoolIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Contributor" \ --scope "$nodeRgId"

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$agentPoolIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Network Contributor" \ --scope "$vnSubnetId"
15. Download the cluster's *kubeconfig* file:
16. az aks get-credentials -n $clusterName -g $rg
17. Clone the *virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances* [GitHub repo](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances):
18. git clone https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances.git
19. Install the Virtual Nodes on ACI Helm chart:
20. helm install /Helm/virtualnode
21. Confirm the Virtual Nodes node shows within the cluster and is in a *Ready* state (*virtualnode-n*):
22. $ kubectl get node
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000000 Ready 4h13m v1.33.6 aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000001 Ready 4h13m v1.33.6 virtualnode-0 Ready 162m v1.33.7
23. Delete the previous Virtual Nodes node from the cluster:
24. kubectl delete node virtual-node-aci-linux
25. Test and confirm pod scheduling on Virtual Node:
26. apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod metadata: annotations: name: demo-pod spec: containers:
- command:
- /bin/bash
- -c
- 'counter=1; while true; do echo "Hello, World! Counter: $counter"; counter=$((counter+1)); sleep 1; done'
image: mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli name: hello-world-counter resources: limits: cpu: 2250m memory: 2256Mi requests: cpu: 100m memory: 128Mi nodeSelector: virtualization: virtualnode2 tolerations:
- effect: NoSchedule
key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider operator: Exists

If the pod successfully starts on the Virtual Node, you should see similar to the below:
27. $ kubectl get pod -o wide demo-pod
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES demo-pod 1/1 Running 0 95s 10.241.0.4 vnode2-virtualnode-0

## Modify your deployments to run on Virtual Nodes on ACI

For Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy), the following *nodeSelector* and *tolerations* are used to run pods on Virtual Nodes:

- nodeSelector:
kubernetes.io/role: agent kubernetes.io/os: linux type: virtual-kubelet tolerations:
- key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider
operator: Exists
- key: azure.com/aci
effect: NoSchedule

For Virtual Nodes on ACI, the nodeSelector/tolerations are slightly different:
- nodeSelector:
virtualization: virtualnode2 tolerations:
- effect: NoSchedule
key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider operator: Exists

## Troubleshooting

1. Check the *virtual-node-admission-controller* and *virtualnode-n* pods are running within the *vn2* namespace:
2. $ kubectl get pod -n vn2
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE virtual-node-admission-controller-54cb7568f5-b7hnr 1/1 Running 1 (5h21m ago) 5h21m virtualnode-0 6/6 Running 6 (4h48m ago) 4h51m

If these pods are in a *Pending* state, your node pool(s) may not have enough resources available to schedule them (use *kubectl describe pod* to validate).
3. If the *virtualnode-n* pod is crashing, check the logs of the *proxycri* container to see whether there are any Managed Identity permissions issues (the cluster's *-agentpool* MSI needs to have *Contributor* access on the [infrastructure resource group](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/faq#why-are-two-resource-groups-created-with-aks-)):

``` kubectl logs -n vn2 virtualnode-0 -c proxycri ```

Further troubleshooting guidance is available within the [official documentation](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/Troubleshooting.md).

## Support

If you have issues deploying or using Virtual Nodes on ACI, add a GitHub issue [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/issues)
Title: Migrating to the next generation of Virtual Nodes on Azure Container Instances (ACI)
Author: adamsharif-msft
EnhancedContent:
As a quick recap, Virtual Nodes lets Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) burst pods onto Azure Container Instances (ACI) on‑demand, without managing or scaling extra VM nodes.

In this article I will demonstrate how you can migrate an existing AKS cluster using the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy), to the new generation of Virtual Nodes on ACI, which is deployed and managed via Helm.

More information about Virtual Nodes on Azure Container Instances can be found [here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-instances/container-instances-virtual-nodes), and the GitHub repo is available [here](https://github.com/microsoft/VirtualNodesOnAzureContainerInstances).

Advanced documentation for Virtual Nodes on ACI is also available [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/tree/main/Docs), and includes topics such as node customisation, release notes and a troubleshooting guide.

> >
> **Please note that all code samples within this guide are examples only, and are provided without warranty/support.**
> >

## Background

Virtual Nodes on ACI is rebuilt from the ground-up, and includes several fixes and enhancements, for instance:

**Added support/features**

- VNet peering, outbound traffic to the internet with network security groups
- Init containers
- Host aliases
- Arguments for exec in ACI
- Persistent Volumes and Persistent Volume Claims
- Container hooks
- Confidential containers (see supported regions list [here](https://github.com/microsoft/VirtualNodesOnAzureContainerInstances#region-deployments))
- [ACI standby pools](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/NodeCustomizations.md#standby-pools)

**Planned future enhancements**

- Support for ACR image pull via Service Principal (SPN)
- Kubernetes network policies
- Support for IPv6
- Windows containers
- Port Forwarding

Note: The new generation of the add-on is managed via Helm rather than as an AKS managed add-on.

### Limitations

- DaemonSets are not supported
- Virtual Nodes on ACI requires AKS clusters with Azure CNI networking (Kubenet is not supported)
- Incompatible with API server authorized IP ranges for AKS (because of the subnet delegation to ACI)

### Requirements

- Each Virtual Nodes on ACI deployment requires 3 vCPUs and 12 GiB memory on one of the AKS cluster’s VMs.
- Each Virtual Nodes on ACI deployment supports up to 200 pods.

# Deploying the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy)

For the sake of completeness, I will first guide you through the traditional steps of deploying the Virtual Nodes managed add-on for AKS.

For this walkthrough, I'm using Bash via [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL),](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) along with the [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli).

These steps are adapted from the official documentation [here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/virtual-nodes-cli)

1. Set up environment variables:

``` location=northeurope rg=rg-virtualnode-demo vnetName=vnet-virtualnode-demo clusterName=aks-virtualnode-demo aksSubnetName=subnet-aks vnSubnetName=subnet-vn ```
2. Create resource group for the cluster and VNet:

``` az group create --name $rg --location $location ```
3. Create Virtual Network (VNet) and AKS/ACI subnets:

``` az network vnet create \ --resource-group $rg --name $vnetName \ --address-prefixes 10.0.0.0/8 \ --subnet-name $aksSubnetName \ --subnet-prefix 10.240.0.0/16

az network vnet subnet create \ --resource-group $rg \ --vnet-name $vnetName \ --name $vnSubnetName \ --address-prefixes 10.241.0.0/16 \ --delegations Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups ```
4. Retrieve the resource IDs for the AKS and ACI subnets:

``` az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $aksSubnetName --query id -o tsv subnetId=$(az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $aksSubnetName --query id -o tsv) vnSubnetId=$(az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $vnSubnetName --query id -o tsv) ```
5. Create a small AKS cluster with 2 nodes:

``` az aks create --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName \ --node-count 2 --node-osdisk-size 30 --node-vm-size Standard_B4ms \ --network-plugin azure --vnet-subnet-id $subnetId \ --generate-ssh-keys ```
6. Enable the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy):

``` az aks enable-addons --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --addons virtual-node --subnet-name $vnSubnetName ```
7. Retrieve the Managed Identity (MSI) used by Virtual Nodes and assign it the *Network Contributor* role for the ACI subnet:

``` vnIdentityId=$(az aks show \ --resource-group $rg \ --name $clusterName \ --query "addonProfiles.aciConnectorLinux.identity.resourceId" \ -o tsv)

vnIdentityObjectId=$(az identity show --ids $vnIdentityId --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$vnIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Network Contributor" \ --scope "$vnSubnetId" ```
8. Download the cluster's *kubeconfig* file:

``` az aks get-credentials --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName ```
9. Confirm the Virtual Nodes node shows within the cluster and is in a *Ready* state (*virtual-node-aci-linux*):

``` $ kubectl get node NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000000 Ready <none> 46m v1.33.6 aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000001 Ready <none> 46m v1.33.6 virtual-node-aci-linux Ready agent 3m28s v1.25.0-vk-azure-aci-1.6.2 ```

## Migrating to the next generation of Virtual Nodes on Azure Container Instances via Helm chart

I will now explain how to migrate from the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy) to the new generation of Virtual Nodes on ACI.

For this walkthrough, I'm using Bash via [Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL),](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) along with the [Azure CLI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure/install-azure-cli).

Direct migration is not supported, and therefore the steps below show an example of removing Virtual Nodes managed add-on and its resources and then installing the Virtual Nodes on ACI Helm chart.

In this walkthrough I will explain how to delete and re-create the Virtual Nodes subnet, however if you need to preserve the VNet and/or use a custom subnet name, refer to the Helm customisation steps [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/NodeCustomizations.md#default-aci-subnet-behaviors-with-a-customized-acisubnetname).

1. Initialise environment variables

``` location=northeurope rg=rg-virtualnode-demo vnetName=vnet-virtualnode-demo clusterName=aks-virtualnode-demo aksSubnetName=subnet-aks vnSubnetName=subnet-vn ```
2. Scale-down any running Virtual Nodes workloads (example below):

``` kubectl delete deploy <deploymentName> -n <namespace> ```
3. Disable the Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy):

``` az aks disable-addons --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --addons virtual-node ```
4. Export a backup of the original subnet configuration:

``` az network vnet subnet show --resource-group $rg --vnet-name $vnetName --name $vnSubnetName > subnetConfigOriginal.json ```
5. Delete the original subnet (subnets cannot be renamed and therefore must be re-created):

``` az network vnet subnet delete -g $rg -n $vnSubnetName --vnet-name $vnetName ```
6. Create the new Virtual Nodes on ACI subnet (replicate the configuration of the original subnet but with the specific name value of *cg*):

``` vnSubnetId=$(az network vnet subnet create \ --resource-group $rg \ --vnet-name $vnetName \ --name cg \ --address-prefixes 10.241.0.0/16 \ --delegations Microsoft.ContainerInstance/containerGroups --query id -o tsv) ```
7. Assign the cluster's *-kubelet* identity *Contributor* access to the infrastructure resource group, and *Network Contributor* access to the ACI subnet:

``` nodeRg=$(az aks show --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --query nodeResourceGroup -o tsv) nodeRgId=$(az group show -n $nodeRg --query id -o tsv)

agentPoolIdentityId=$(az aks show --resource-group $rg --name $clusterName --query "identityProfile.kubeletidentity.resourceId" -o tsv)

agentPoolIdentityObjectId=$(az identity show --ids $agentPoolIdentityId --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$agentPoolIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Contributor" \ --scope "$nodeRgId"

az role assignment create \ --assignee-object-id "$agentPoolIdentityObjectId" \ --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \ --role "Network Contributor" \ --scope "$vnSubnetId" ```
8. Download the cluster's *kubeconfig* file:

``` az aks get-credentials -n $clusterName -g $rg ```
9. Clone the *virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances* [GitHub repo](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances):

``` git clone https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances.git ```
10. Install the Virtual Nodes on ACI Helm chart:

``` helm install <yourReleaseName> <GitRepoRoot>/Helm/virtualnode ```
11. Confirm the Virtual Nodes node shows within the cluster and is in a *Ready* state (*virtualnode-n*):

``` $ kubectl get node NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000000 Ready <none> 4h13m v1.33.6 aks-nodepool1-35702456-vmss000001 Ready <none> 4h13m v1.33.6 virtualnode-0 Ready <none> 162m v1.33.7 ```
12. Delete the previous Virtual Nodes node from the cluster:

``` kubectl delete node virtual-node-aci-linux ```
13. Test and confirm pod scheduling on Virtual Node:

``` apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: annotations: name: demo-pod spec: containers:
- command:
- /bin/bash
- -c
- 'counter=1; while true; do echo "Hello, World! Counter: $counter"; counter=$((counter+1)); sleep 1; done'
image: mcr.microsoft.com/azure-cli name: hello-world-counter resources: limits: cpu: 2250m memory: 2256Mi requests: cpu: 100m memory: 128Mi nodeSelector: virtualization: virtualnode2 tolerations:
- effect: NoSchedule
key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider operator: Exists ```

If the pod successfully starts on the Virtual Node, you should see similar to the below:

``` $ kubectl get pod -o wide demo-pod NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODE READINESS GATES demo-pod 1/1 Running 0 95s 10.241.0.4 vnode2-virtualnode-0 <none> <none> ```

## Modify your deployments to run on Virtual Nodes on ACI

For Virtual Nodes managed add-on (legacy), the following *nodeSelector* and *tolerations* are used to run pods on Virtual Nodes:

``` nodeSelector: kubernetes.io/role: agent kubernetes.io/os: linux type: virtual-kubelet tolerations:
- key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider
operator: Exists
- key: azure.com/aci
effect: NoSchedule ```

For Virtual Nodes on ACI, the nodeSelector/tolerations are slightly different:

``` nodeSelector: virtualization: virtualnode2 tolerations:
- effect: NoSchedule
key: virtual-kubelet.io/provider operator: Exists ```

## Troubleshooting

1. Check the *virtual-node-admission-controller* and *virtualnode-n* pods are running within the *vn2* namespace:

``` $ kubectl get pod -n vn2 NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE virtual-node-admission-controller-54cb7568f5-b7hnr 1/1 Running 1 (5h21m ago) 5h21m virtualnode-0 6/6 Running 6 (4h48m ago) 4h51m ```

If these pods are in a *Pending* state, your node pool(s) may not have enough resources available to schedule them (use *kubectl describe pod* to validate).
2. If the *virtualnode-n* pod is crashing, check the logs of the *proxycri* container to see whether there are any Managed Identity permissions issues (the cluster's *-agentpool* MSI needs to have *Contributor* access on the [infrastructure resource group](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/faq#why-are-two-resource-groups-created-with-aks-)):

``` kubectl logs -n vn2 virtualnode-0 -c proxycri ```

Further troubleshooting guidance is available within the [official documentation](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/blob/main/Docs/Troubleshooting.md).

## Support

If you have issues deploying or using Virtual Nodes on ACI, add a GitHub issue [here](https://github.com/microsoft/virtualnodesOnAzureContainerInstances/issues)
